source.transcript:
Meeting header: 2027-02-11 15:00 PST | Duration: ~52m | Attendees: Samira (Redwood AE), Ethan (Redwood SE), Dana (CTO), Miles (SRE), Priya (PM)

[00:00] Samira Lopez: Okay everyone, thanks for joining. quick agenda — quick sync on the last 48 hours of errors, i have the pilot telemetry up, then ethan will walk through the proposed circuit-breaker/playbook, and we close with next steps.
[00:18] Dana Torres: Yeah thanks, we had two medium-sized spikes, one yesterday at 10:37am where a bunch of requests to /generate hit 502s and then again at 3am this morning with timeouts. We're seeing tail latency shoot up to the low seconds at P99 which breaks our internal SLA.
[00:35] Miles Chen: to add, the 5xx pattern looks correlated with a warmup window — when we scale up instances, the kv cache is cold and the latency climbs. i'm not sure if it's the model load or a network-ish thing.
[00:50] Ethan Park: got it. so prelim telemetry from your traces shows long first-token latencies during those scale events; also we observed a bunch of client-side retries happening in tight loops which amplified backpressure.
[01:10] Priya Rao: side note — our product SLA says P95 < 300ms on short prompts; so even a small tail affects the UX.
[01:22] Samira Lopez: thanks. ok ethan, can you walk through the circuit-breaker change set you mentioned in the doc?
[01:28] Ethan Park: sure. so currently the pilot is using a fairly permissive cb: threshold 10% 5xx over 1m window before tripping. we propose tightening to 5% over 30s for the critical /generate route, and add an open duration of 45s with fast half-open probing, plus a sliding-window P99 check that will force a soft-fallback to a smaller model if tail latency crosses 1.2s.
[01:49] Miles Chen: are we worried about too-aggressive tripping causing false positives during legitimate bursts? we can't have cb flip on and off.
[01:56] Ethan Park: yeah that's the tradeoff. to mitigate flapping, we suggest a backoff multiplier on the cb's reopen delay (x2 after each fail up to 8m) and use token-based sampling to allow a steady stream of health probes even when open. also client retry budgets: move from unlimited immediate retries to a leaky-bucket budget of 3 retries with jittered exponential backoff starting at 150ms.
[02:22] Dana Torres: that sounds reasonable. can you explain the fallback path? you mentioned smaller model — is that served by the same pool?
[02:29] Ethan Park: fallback is to a quantized variant in the same region we pre-warm; it's compatible and faster but slightly lower quality. the runtime supports automatic prefix/KV cache warmup when we route traffic to the fallback pool to avoid cold-start latencies. note that 'kv cache' might get transcribed weirdly in our notes sometimes as 'kv cash' or 'kv-cache' — apologies.
[02:47] Speaker 1: (mumble) what's the impact on cost if we route to fallback?
[02:51] Samira Lopez: fallback saves cost per token in heavy loads because the smaller model consumes fewer GPU cycles; but there is an ops cost for keeping a warm standby pool. we'll include approx numbers in the pricing doc.
[03:05] Miles Chen: for the 5xxs we pulled, there are two stacks — most are internal 502 type responses, some are 504 timeouts. attached traces show a lot of client retry storms exactly at the same minute.
[03:20] Ethan Park: that's why we want to enforce client-side retry budgets. if clients hammer retries with no backoff, it basically becomes a thundering herd and trips upstream. Redwood can emit guidance and a lightweight client library with recommended retry semantics.
[03:36] Dana Torres: we'll need that guidance. our frontend team has been doing naive retries.
[03:40] Priya Rao: also can we set a per-route rate-limit window that's slightly lower than what the Premium pool can handle so that we get graceful degradation before backend overload?
[03:49] Ethan Park: yes, route-level soft-limits + a circuit breaker cascade is our recommended pattern. soft-limit returns 429 with retry-after header and is much nicer than 502/504. Redwood console will show token-level breakdowns so you can tune window size; we can propose a starting window of 120 requests/min for the high-traffic endpoints.
[04:12] Samira Lopez: ok practical plan — we will: send pricing/burst policy, provide the retry playbook, and ethan will run the replay test. miles will share consolidated traces.
[04:20] Miles Chen: noted. on traces, one ask: can redwood accept a zip of flamegraphs and traces? we'll drop in S3.
[04:26] Ethan Park: yes. ideally compressed traces (zip or .tar.gz) with request ids. we'll correlate with our sidecar/tags.
[04:34] Dana Torres: security question: any issues with sending traces that contain snippets of prompts? we have PHI in certain prompts for the POC.
[04:42] Samira Lopez: good call. please scrub PHI before sending or use the secure upload link we'll provide. Redwood is SOC 2 Type II, supports customer-managed KMS for private deployments, and we can redact or provide a sanctioned pipeline for trace ingestion.
[04:59] Priya Rao: can we confirm SSO/SAML for console access during the POC?
[05:03] Samira Lopez: yes, SAML SSO is available for the pilot, we just need SP metadata. we'll follow up with the SSO checklist.
[05:10] Ethan Park: back to technicals — i want to call out tail-latency sources: model tokenization overhead, KV cache misses, and transient GC in the runtime container. for the kv-cache warmup we can send synthetic prefix traffic to seed the cache before peak windows. that usually knocks down first-token P99.
[05:34] Miles Chen: synthetic traffic? how do we avoid it triggering rate-limits?
[05:38] Ethan Park: we'd tag synthetic traffic with a special header so it can be routed to a warmup pool and not counted against your production quota, or we run it at low priority. Samira will include warmup script / example in the repo.
[05:52] Speaker 1: (inaudible) can you also add a health endpoint?
[05:55] Ethan Park: yes, we expose per-model health and a readiness probe, also a /telemetry endpoint for aggregated stats.
[06:03] Samira Lopez: ok, let's talk about replay test specifics — ethan?
[06:07] Ethan Park: we propose a 10k request replay of a sampled 24h window that produced the spike, replayed at pace to reproduce backpressure — we will run it in the Redwood staging cluster pointed at your pilot pool with the new circuit-breaker and retry settings. outcome metrics: P95, P99, error rate, queue length, and rate of cb openings.
[06:27] Dana Torres: timeline?
[06:29] Ethan Park: i can kick it off after i get the trace bundle; estimate 4-6 hours to run and analyze. will post a short report.
[06:37] Priya Rao: what if replay improves tail but user experience still suffers?
[06:41] Ethan Park: then fallback routing and increasingly aggressive cb thresholds are our knobs — also consider a conditional model-quantization profile for heavy traffic windows. Redwood Optimize can suggest quant profiles based on your workload shapes.
[06:55] Miles Chen: can we get a config we can apply via API so our infra team can toggle thresholds without redeploy?
[07:00] Samira Lopez: yes, config is dynamic through the console and API — we'll send sample curl snippets.
[07:07] Dana Torres: any regulatory concerns with fallback models? sometimes we need a deterministically reviewed model for compliance.
[07:13] Ethan Park: if needed, fallback can be to a certified variant pinned to a specific quantization/version — audit logs will show rollouts and fallbacks. that can satisfy regulatory requirements.
[07:29] Samira Lopez: ok wrapping to action items and owners. Miles will send traces, Samira will send pricing and SSO checklist, Ethan will run replay, Priya will confirm endpoints SLAs.
[07:41] Priya Rao: confirmed. can you also add a note about client library and recommended retry code snippet?
[07:46] Ethan Park: yes, i'll add code in the repo — pseudo: exponential backoff, jitter, max 3 retries, don't retry on 4xx except 429.
[07:58] Samira Lopez: any blockers?
[08:01] Miles Chen: just time to scrub traces and internal approvals for PHI redaction.
[08:06] Dana Torres: ok, we can have the scrubbed bundle to you by EOD Friday.
[08:10] Samira Lopez: great. last check — do we want the blue/green canary next week if replay looks good?
[08:16] Priya Rao: yes, book it.
[08:18] Samira Lopez: perfect. thanks everyone. we'll follow up with the doc links and the test plan.
[08:24] (meeting ends)

Notes: transcription has a few mis-heard words (kv cache sometimes as 'kv cash'), speaker labels include a miscellaneous 'Speaker 1' for an internal attendee. The recording duration ~52 minutes; timestamps here are approximate markers for dialogue chunks and not exact second-for-second transcript.

Referenced artifacts / links:
- Pilot telemetry dashboard (Redwood console) — link: https://console.redwood.ai/pilot/nimbus/telemetry (placeholder)
- Replay test plan (gdoc) — link: https://redwood.in/rep-playbook (placeholder)
- Pricing + burst policy draft (will be attached by Samira)
- SSO checklist (will be attached by Samira)

End of transcript.

source.transcript:
Header:
Date: 2026-09-02 15:00 UTC
Duration: 48 minutes
Attendees: Ava Chen (Redwood AE), Diego Martinez (Redwood SE), Maya Patel (Nimbus CTO), Samir Rao (Eng Lead), Elena Ortiz (SRE), Jon K (Product), Heather (Procurement)
Links referenced: deck - https://redwood.link/nimbus-pilot-deck , benchmark sheet - https://sheets.redwood/nimbus-bench-2026

Summary: quick reliability postmortem and tuning session after intermittent 5xx/timeouts during token-burst windows; focused on client retry budgets, server-side short-window rate limiting, and fallback routing to canary model variant.

Topics: retry budget, adaptive backoff, burst window, KV cache warming, canary fallback model, Dedicated vs Hosted economics, runbook for incident escalation

Transcript:
00:00 Ava (Redwood AE): Okay everyone, thanks for joining, hopefully audio is good. we had a busy 72 hours so wanted to walk through the telemetry and make decisions on retry/backoff and fallbacks. Maya you had the priority context?
00:37 Maya (Nimbus CTO): Yeah thanks, um we saw a spike of 502s starting around 03:12Z on Sept 1st, it lasted intermittently for about 45 minutes, overall latency jumped and some client jobs timed out. Those were our nightly anonymization batches, they burst thousands of tokens in a short window.
01:05 Diego (Redwood SE): Right, our logs line up — the 5xxs are coming from an overload in the inference queue; the burst exceeded the per-pod token acceptance window. We saw some long tail where KV cache wasn't warmed for those specific prompts.
01:34 Samir (Nimbus Eng Lead): Quick Q — when you say token acceptance window, is that per-connection or per-GPU? Like can we smooth client spikes without capacity changes?
01:51 Diego (Redwood SE): It's a combo. There's the per-worker queue and batching chamber on the GPU. If many large requests hit simultaneously they compete for batching slots; that produces backpressure and some requests time out upstream. We can mitigate in two places: client-side throttle & retry budgeting, and server-side short window rate-limiter that smooths token bursts across rack.
02:20 Elena (Nimbus SRE): We tried increasing client timeouts but that masked failures and increased concurrency. We prefer to avoid long timeouts because it ties up threads in our app.
02:38 Ava (Redwood AE): Good note. So proposed plan: short-term — tune client retry budget (fewer immediate retries, exponential backoff with jitter), implement a server-side token-window limiter for bursts, and add a canary fallback route to a smaller model to preserve availability. Diego can outline the tradeoffs.
03:00 Diego (Redwood SE): Sure. So fallback to a smaller model (say a 7B quantized variant) reduces cost and latency but might degrade output quality for specific prompts. We recommend A/Bing the fallback: for non-critical endpoints send fallback, for high-quality routes preserve stronger retries or route to Dedicated pool. Also add a per-customer burst token window, e.g., 10k tokens per 30s with a leaky bucket. That prevents the queue from filling instantaneously.
03:35 Maya (Nimbus CTO): Leaky bucket sounds good, can you make it tunable per route? Our billing has high variance and some teams legitimately burst.
03:52 Diego (Redwood SE): Yes, it's per-route and per-account configurable. Defaults are conservative. We can expose it in the pilot config UI and via the API.
04:05 Jon K (Product): Is there a quick experiment we can run to see effect without impacting users?
04:12 Diego (Redwood SE): We can do a controlled load test from Nimbus CI hitting the canary with the burst window enabled and then compare 5xx rate, p50/p95 latencies in the benchmark sheet.
04:29 Ava (Redwood AE): Action: Nimbus to run a controlled burst on Wed 9/8 and send logs. Diego will provision canary. I'll send the tuned retry doc. Heather — you'll need the pricing snapshot for procurement.
04:45 Heather (Procurement): Yep I'll need committed vs pay-as-you-go numbers and the Dedicated bump.
05:00 Elena (SRE): One thing — our logs show retries coming from the client at 0ms, like instant retries. Can you provide recommended client-side config?
05:14 Ava (Redwood AE): Absolutely. We'll share a sample: initial delay 150-250ms, exponential factor 1.8-2.2, max attempts 3-4 and jitter, and special casing for idempotent vs non-idempotent routes.
05:36 Diego (Redwood SE): Also watch for retry storms: if all clients retry in sync it'll recreate bursts. Add randomized jitter and possibly use 503 with Retry-After when we're intentionally shedding.
05:58 Samir (Eng Lead): Curious — can Redwood emit Retry-After headers?
06:04 Diego (Redwood SE): Yes we can, on Hosted we will add a Retry-After numeric header; for Dedicated it's configurable. When we return 429/503 we can include Retry-After seconds which your client can honor.
06:22 Maya (CTO): About KV cache — you mentioned warming. Is that something we can pre-warm for nightly jobs?
06:31 Diego (Redwood SE): We can pre-warm via a lightweight warmup request pattern; we recommend a small warmup set that hits common prefixes. For big batch jobs you can submit a warm-up phase 5-10 minutes ahead of the job to seed the KV cache. That lowered 5xx in similar customers.
06:54 Elena (SRE): We'll try a warmup. We'll include 100 representative prompts.
07:03 Ava (Redwood AE): Diego, economics: if Nimbus switches heavy traffic to Dedicated, what's the likely latency delta and cost ballpark?
07:14 Diego (Redwood SE): Dedicated lowers noisy neighbor effects, you get better p95 and stable throughput. Cost varies by commit; I'll include a snapshot in the pricing doc but expect ~30-40% improvement in p95 for same model under high burst loads. We'll show committed tier vs on-demand numbers.
07:38 Jon (Product): Can we get fallback fidelity numbers? Like quality drop from primary to fallback model?
07:45 Diego (Redwood SE): We ran quick evals on similar prompt sets — fallback produced 12-18% score drop on our automated evals for the anonymization task; for many product features that's acceptable since the downstream processes do additional filtering. I'll add details to the benchmark sheet.
08:15 Maya (CTO): Any security or compliance risks with fallback? We want to ensure data residency and audit logs remain intact.
08:25 Ava (Redwood AE): Good point — fallbacks still route through your configured tenant with audit logs and KMS if enabled. We'll confirm that in the runbook.
08:38 Elena (SRE): Please include specific log fields we can rely on to correlate client request id -> Redwood request id.
08:46 Diego (Redwood SE): We'll add those fields and sample queries for your SIEM.
09:00 Samir (Eng Lead): On the retry front — our current client impl retries on any 5xx; should we narrow that?
09:08 Diego (Redwood SE): Yes narrow to idempotent ops and 502/504 transient errors; avoid retrying on 400/422. Also backoff more aggressively if you see successive 5xx in a short span.
09:30 Maya (CTO): We also had a question about rate-limits being per-account vs per-route earlier. Can we get both?
09:38 Ava (Redwood AE): Yes, you can configure both. We'll send recommended defaults in the doc.
09:45 Jon (Product): Ooh can Redwood provide a policy that does model fallback only after X retries to preserve quality on first attempts?
09:52 Diego (Redwood SE): Yes, policy-based routing is supported. We can configure fallback after N attempts or after a latency threshold. We'll put a sample policy in the sheet.
10:12 Elena (SRE): In the 5xx logs there were a few correlation IDs with full stack traces that referenced kernel OOMs — is that something on your side?
10:24 Diego (Redwood SE): Kernel OOMs are rare; could be large sequence lengths and memory fragmentation. We'll dig into the exact request traces you send and check if quantized kernels triggered memory pressure. We did push a runtime patch this morning that reduces memory jitter.
10:46 Samir (Eng Lead): Do you want sample request payloads?
10:50 Elena (SRE): We'll attach the anonymized payloads with the log slice.
10:55 Ava (Redwood AE): Great, include request ids and timestamps. Diego will review and propose kernel config changes.
11:05 Diego (Redwood SE): Also FYI if you want to avoid runtime changes we can instead route that job to a Dedicated worker pool for the short window, which is less risky but costs more.
11:20 Heather (Procurement): For procurement — what documentation do you need from us to speed Dedicated evaluation?
11:28 Ava (Redwood AE): Heather, we'll send a short checklist: expected commit, region, SSO SAML, SOC2/ISO needs, and a contact for legal. I'll attach it to the pricing email.
11:40 Heather (Procurement): Perfect.
11:48 Maya (CTO): What about observability? We need p50/p95 token-level breakdown and traces showing batch sizes.
11:56 Diego (Redwood SE): The Console has token-level cost and per-route latency, and we can give a short retention extension for the pilot. We'll add a dashboard link to the sheet.
12:10 Jon (Product): Any quick wins we can do in the next 48 hours?
12:15 Ava (Redwood AE): Yes: 1) change client retry config (we'll send exact snippet), 2) add warmup phase for nightly job, 3) enable short 30s per-account token burst limiter on the Hosted side as a test, 4) Diego will provision canary model.
12:32 Elena (SRE): We'll prioritize warmup and client config change tomorrow.
12:40 Ava (Redwood AE): ok, let's capture action owners and due dates — I'll follow up in email. Anything else?
12:48 Samir (Eng Lead): Micro note — are there any recommended SDK changes? Our SDK is in Go and has naive retry behavior.
12:56 Diego (Redwood SE): We'll paste Go example with jittered exponential backoff, and a helper SDK method that honors Retry-After header. We'll include that in the repo link.
13:12 Maya (CTO): Also want a short runbook for on-call — who do we page at Redwood if we see a production-wide degradation?
13:20 Ava (Redwood AE): We'll share an on-call escalation matrix: AE -> SE -> infra on-call; plus a Redwood status link and your SRE's sync channel.
13:35 Jon (Product): For the experimental fallback, can we run 10% of traffic through fallback for 24 hours to gauge quality?
13:44 Diego (Redwood SE): Yes, can do a canary rollout with early rollback on quality regression alerts. We'll integrate with your A/B metrics.
13:58 Maya (CTO): Ok that covers it, thanks.
14:05 Ava (Redwood AE): Thanks everyone. Quick recap in chat and I'll drop the spreadsheet. Diego: you taking runbook + canary?
14:14 Diego (Redwood SE): Yep, I'll take the canary and runbook bits.
14:20 Ava (Redwood AE): Great. Meeting end.

Notes: occasional transcription artifacts: names like "Diego" were sometimes logged as "Dego" in the raw file; timestamps approximate.

source.transcript:
Meeting header:
Date: 2025-10-21
Start: 15:00 UTC
Duration: ~56 minutes
Attendees: Alex Kim (Redwood AE), Priya Natarajan (Redwood SE), Ben Carter (CSM) - Redwood; Dana Ortiz (ML Lead), Marcus Lee (Infra), Sofia Ruiz (Product), Tom Becker (Data) - Nimbus Health
Links shared: benchmark spreadsheet (drive), regression deck (slides), security FAQ (link)

Summary (auto-gen, may be rough): Quick sync on week-2 lo-bit (int8/fp8) conversion pilot. Reviewed perf deltas, a few regressions on hallucination-sensitive prompts, discussed calibration set and quant params. Agreed next steps to run a longer stability job and share additional failing prompts.

[00:00] Alex: okay hey everyone, thanks for jumping on, we're on for the lo-bit conversion poc weekly, um, objective today is quick results, what changed since last week, any surprises, and next steps. 
[00:28] Dana: great, thanks Alex, hi everyone. we pushed the int8 build into staging last night, ran a few smoke tests, saw throughput improvements but a couple of quality blips on our extraction prompts.
[00:45] Priya: Can you say "extraction prompts" a bit more — are these the structured-medical-record extraction templates?
[00:52] Dana: yeah exactly, like the patient-summary -> medication list parsing. some fields are missing or merged, like "aspirin 81mg" turned into "aspirin81", which breaks downstream parsers.
[01:10] Marcus: from infra we saw p99 token latency drop roughly 22% on average with INT8 with batch size 8, and peak throughput up ~1.8x, but we did notice occasional 500ms spikes at sequence lengths > 1024 when cache warmup happens.
[01:29] Priya: that spike sounds like KV cache population effects. Did you run with prefix caching enabled?
[01:35] Marcus: yes, prefix cache was on, but we had the per-route cache TTL low and some cache thrashing when many sessions hit similar prefixes.
[01:48] Priya: okay makes sense. For the quality issues: did you run symmetric vs asymmetric quantization, and what about group-wise quant?
[01:56] Dana: we used the default conversion script that Alex sent — I think it was symmetric per-tensor? maybe per-channel for some layers, not sure. the sheet says "autofit" which might be Redwood's auto heuristics.
[02:14] Alex: yeah the conversion used our auto-mode: per-channel for weights where applicable, but activation quant was symmetric with a 8-bit clip; it may not be ideal for those NER-like extraction tokens. we also tried an FP8 build in parallel and the FP8 preserved a lot more of the tokenization boundaries, less merging.
[02:36] Sofia: interesting. so FP8 looked better for QA/extraction, but INT8 showed better throughput — is that the quick TL;DR?
[02:44] Priya: short answer yes. FP8 sits in a sweet spot for quality on those sensitive prompts but its perf gains are smaller, depends on kernel support on the hardware. our INF card supports FP8 fast paths for a subset of ops so the delta was about +1.15x on throughput, but INT8 gave +1.8x.
[03:04] Dana: ok, can you quantify the quality drop — what metrics are we looking at? we have accuracy and a custom entity-F1.
[03:11] Priya: we ran the 2.5k prompt validation set: for INT8 we saw entity-F1 drop from 0.92 to 0.86 on average, with a tail of prompts down to 0.75. FP8 was 0.91 average. perplexity doesn't capture the issue here because it's a tokenization boundary error.
[03:35] Tom: we also instrumented the pipeline and saw that the string post-processing regexes were tripped by missing spaces, so that's a brittle point.
[03:43] Ben: is there a quick remediation for those cases? maybe post-quantization token repair heuristics?
[03:50] Priya: a few options: 1) recalibration with a representative calibration set that includes those extraction examples, 2) per-layer higher precision (mixed-bit), 3) FP8 on sensitive routes only, or 4) software fallback for those prompt patterns.
[04:08] Dana: can we do mixed-bit on only the final attention layers?
[04:13] Priya: yes, we can selectively keep some layers in fp16 while quantizing others to int8. that often recovers most of the quality with modest perf loss. we saw in the sandbox that keeping the last two transformer blocks at fp16 recovered entity-F1 to 0.90 while throughput settled at ~1.4x.
[04:33] Marcus: operationally, switching routing to FP8 for a subset of model variants — how heavy is that? can we do that retargeting with a rollout?
[04:40] Alex: yes, Redwood routing can map by policy: e.g., prompt route or customer feature flag to FP8 variant. you can do canary rollouts and automatic fallback to fp16/int8 if latency or error thresholds trigger. I'll attach an example policy.
[04:56] Dana: cool. a question on calibration: how big should the calibration corpus be? we used 2k examples but maybe that's too small or too biased.
[05:05] Priya: recommended is 8k-10k diverse samples for our models for stable clipping statistics, especially for per-channel quant. We'll re-run with the 10k set and include those rare extraction edge-cases.
[05:20] Tom: I can assemble a 10k set from production anonymized records, but need to know format and redaction guidance.
[05:26] Alex: we have a redaction checklist, I'll forward. Ben, can you also share the drive folder link?
[05:31] Ben: yep I'll put the link in the notes. (link pasted)
[05:40] [06:00] Dana: about the hallucination regressions — we noticed when forced decoding of structured fields the model would sometimes invent measurements. Did you see that with FP8?
[06:10] Priya: less with FP8, more with INT8. I think some activation clipping can nudge logits and change the argmax on boundary cases. This shows up as hallucinated numeric values in the extraction outputs.
[06:23] Sofia: is this a function of tokenizer mismatch or quant noise?
[06:27] Priya: mostly quant-induced perturbation. tokenizer mismatch would be more consistent across builds.
[06:33] Dana: ok, we care more about correctness than absolute latency; our SLO is request p95 < 350ms for end-to-end but accuracy is the highest priority.
[06:44] Alex: noted — we can propose a hybrid policy: critical routes map to FP8 or mixed-bit while non-critical analytics routes can use INT8. Also cost math: INT8 reduced token cost by ~38%, FP8 about 22% in our model.

[09:54] Priya: ok we'll add the hook instructions to the notes.

[10:19] Dana: thanks.

[10:34] Ben: I'll also attach the benchmark sheet and raw logs.

[10:40] Alex: thanks everyone, talk soon.

source.transcript:
Meeting header:
Date: 2025-10-07
Start: 15:00 PT
Duration: 48 minutes (approx)
Attendees: Ethan (Redwood AE), Kira (Redwood CSM), Oliver (Redwood SE), Samir (Redwood Product), Maya (Nimbus Head of ML), Liam (Nimbus CTO), Priya (Nimbus Finance), Alex (Nimbus Platform)

Summary (auto-gen - may be rough): QBR focused on cost spike in Sep, tail latencies on multimodal pipelines, and readiness for private/dedicated capacity in H1 2026. Action items listed.

[00:00] Ethan: Hey everyone, thanks for joining — appreciate you making time for the QBR. We've got about 45 minutes on the agenda. Quick round-robin intros if we need them, otherwise we'll jump in.
[00:12] Maya: Hi, Maya here. I'll be leading for Nimbus on the ML side. We've got some infra folks and finance on too, so hopefully we can cover the cost / reliability bits.
[00:18] Liam: Liam — CTO. Short on time so we'll try to be efficient.
[00:23] Kira: Kira, Customer Success — I'll surface usage trends and follow up on the SLO asks.
[00:28] Oliver: Oliver, SE — I'll have the bench numbers and clarifications on the KV cache config.
[00:31] Priya: Priya from Finance — mostly here for cost questions.
[00:35] Alex: Alex, Platform eng. I'll be coordinating any infra work for the private/bare-metal trial.

[01:02] Ethan: Ok first slide — usage trend overview. Kira, do you want to walk through monthly tokens and embedding units?
[01:10] Kira: Sure. So overall tokens are up 32% Q/Q driven by the new summarization workflow rolled out mid-August. Embedding calls increased 4x in Sep due to the search reindexing job that was inadvertently left in high frequency. That reindexing is the big driver of the cost spike Priya saw. We highlighted the top-ish routes — search-embed-v2, summarizer-prod.
[01:38] Priya: Right that reindex was the issue. Was that a scheduled cron? I thought we had a rate limit.
[01:44] Alex: It was a data team job — they triggered a full reindex after schema changes, but pushed parallelism too high. We didn't catch the rate.
[01:50] Maya: The job used batch size ~512 and didn't use KV cache at all — it was a fresh cold run.
[01:55] Oliver: Yeah that explains low cache hit and high tokens/spend. We'll flag that route for caching and batching suggestions. We can stub in a prefix cache to catch repeated contexts during reindex.
[02:12] Liam: Priya is asking for a concrete dollar delta for that week.
[02:18] Ethan: We have a quick calc — mid-September spike was roughly $18k incremental over the baseline week. I'll put the per-route CSV in the follow up.
[02:25] Priya: That's useful. For budgeting we need to understand sustained run rate if the reindex is repeated.
[02:32] Kira: We recommended a quota and alert on route-level QPS for any non-prod tokens; also suggest rate-limiting the job and switching to a cached embedding pipeline.
[02:42] Maya: Also want to discuss the latency issue. In late September we saw 95th pct latency creep on long-context generation. Tail spiked to ~1.2s per token which impacted our chat UI timeouts.
[02:55] Oliver: Two things there — first the model variant in production was a higher precision non-quantized llm-3b-large variant; second the KV cache hit rate dropped because requests changed format and bypassed the cache key.
[03:09] Maya: Right — we had a prompt templating change which mutated prefix fingerprints.
[03:14] Samir: The product note there is that we can add a fingerprinting helper in the SDK to normalize prefixes so caching is effective. We'll put that on the SDK backlog.
[03:23] Liam: For SLOs what are we proposing? Current SLO is 99.5 p99 token latency < 250ms? That's been hard to meet.
[03:33] Kira: We think splitting by route makes sense. For chat routes set p95 < 300ms and p99 < 900ms for the next quarter with an improvement plan to tighten to 700ms p99 over time. For long-context generation, allow higher token latency but target improved KV-hit > 85%.
[03:54] Priya: If we loosen SLOs, is there a cost implication? We need to signal to finance if downgrading SLO means cheaper hosting.
[04:03] Ethan: There's a trade-off — looser SLOs let us batch more aggressively and use cheaper quantized variants which drop cost per token. We can model two scenarios in that billing export.
[04:15] Maya: Can you quantify 'cheaper'? ballpark percent?
[04:18] Oliver: From our bench: switching from fp16 to 8-bit quantized for that workload brings ~30-40% per-token compute reduction; combined with better batching/caching you can get to ~50% lower infra spend on that route. But there's a small impact on output distribution, need to validate on your eval set.
[04:38] Liam: We'll want a quality regression run before we flip quantized in prod.
[04:41] Samir: Agreed — propose a two-week canary with automated evals against your holdout prompts and rollback on regressions threshold.
[04:49] Maya: Sounds good. Who owns the eval harness?
[04:52] Kira: We can host an eval on Redwood Optimize and share dashboards. We need sample prompts and acceptance criteria from your ML team.
[05:00] Maya: I'll pull together ~200 seed prompts that represent long-tail and happy path.
[05:04] Liam: Next — reliability incidents. There were two incidents in September: a 15-min outage due to a regional network blip and a cascading throttling event when the reindex coincided with a scheduled model redeploy.
[05:18] Oliver: Right, the redeploy triggered a transient drop in capacity and the autoscaler reacted but there was a throttle window. We're looking into faster warmup and a reserved burst queue to avoid that.
[05:28] Alex: For private deployments — we want predictable capacity so redeploys won't impact the rest of our infra. Can you outline the dedicated/reserved capacity options?
[05:36] Ethan: Sure — dedicated reserved pools are the better fit for predictable throughput. We offer monthly reserved capacity with burst credits. You can also pin model variants to the pool and have rolling updates with canary policies. 
[05:48] Maya: We're evaluating private (VPC) vs Dedicated — private gives us control over networking and enclave KMS integration; dedicated hosted gives less up-front operational burden. Timeline for private deploy availability?
[05:59] Samir: For Nimbus-sized customers, private (customer-managed VPC + control plane) is on track mid-H1 2026. We can prep an early readiness review and a proof-of-concept in a partner region earlier if you need it sooner.
[06:12] Liam: Security questions — Priya is on for this — where are keys stored? KMS? HSM? Audit logs?
[06:18] Priya: We have to map to our internal compliance needs; SOC 2 is required, and we need to confirm KMS integration for key rotation and ability to do customer-managed keys.
[06:26] Ethan: Redwood supports customer-managed KMS (Cloud KMS or external HSM via KMIP) for private deployments and we can provide audit logs retention options. For hosted, we provide KMS managed keys but also have a bring-your-own-key path for enterprise.
[06:39] Priya: Do you have a public SOC2 report?
[06:41] Kira: Yes — we'll link the SOC2 and ISO artifacts in the follow up, plus the compliance matrix.
[06:47] Maya: A short tangent — we saw a small semantic drift in generated summaries after an uprev of the tokenizer library. Was that expected?
[06:56] Oliver: We did push a tokenizer fix which changed some tokenization on edge cases; normally no semantic changes, but it's possible with prompt sensitivity to see small deltas. We'll run a regression on your samples.
[07:08] Liam: Ok on the rollout plan: if we do the quantized canary, what's rollback time and what monitoring hooks do you suggest?
[07:16] Oliver: Rollback is instant from the serving control plane. We'll set threshold hooks on Rouge/BLEU-like similarity (internal metric), throughput, and error rates. If similarity drops below X% or error rates rise we auto-fail the canary.
[07:30] Maya: For the eval thresholds, we prefer false negatives over false positives — meaning prefer to fail on quality drops even if it means more manual triage.
[07:40] Samir: We'll make the default threshold conservative. We can also run A/B alongside and provide side-by-side diffs in the console.
[07:48] Priya: Back to cost modeling — we need a 12-month forecast under two scenarios: (A) maintain hosted with quotas and stricter alerts; (B) move critical routes to dedicated/reserved capacity plus private for sensitive data.
[08:02] Ethan: We'll produce both scenarios with assumptions: traffic growth 20% Q/Q, embedding reindex avoided, quantized flips for summarizer route. Expect scenario B to have higher base commitment but lower marginal cost and predictability.
[08:15] Alex: For the infra team can you provide recommended instance sizes and GPU profiles for the dedicated pool?
[08:22] Oliver: Yes — we'll include recommended GPU types (A100x4 vs H100x8 equivalence), batch sizing guidance, and kernel-level opt suggestions. Also a note on how sequence length distribution changes kernel selection and memory footprint.
[08:36] Maya: One more mental note — data residency. A few of our clinics are in EU so we need EU-only processing for those tenants. How is multi-region routing handled?
[08:45] Ethan: We support region pinning and routing policies that let you route EU traffic to EU-only instances. For private, you can host in your EU region or partner cloud. We'll add documentation links in the follow up.
[08:57] Liam: Great. Let's go to the recap and owners.
[09:02] Kira: Recap: Redwood provides billing export and per-route heatmap; SE team runs KV-cache bench and batching bench; Product + Customer agree on a quantized canary and evaluation harness; security artifacts to be shared; private-deploy readiness review scheduled.
[09:20] Priya: Anything else on procurement/legal? We might need updated DPA language for private.
[09:26] Ethan: We have a standard DPA and we can engage Legal to add clauses for private deployments. I'll connect Pam from legal in the thread.
[09:34] Maya: Timeline: we'll aim to review the billing export by 10/9, provide evaluation prompts by 10/13, and have the readiness call week of 10/20.
[09:44] Liam: Fine by me. Thanks everyone.
[09:48] Ethan: Thanks — we'll follow up with the artifacts and set the readiness call.
[09:52] (meeting ends)

Notes on transcript quality: auto-transcript had some mislabels and a couple of words transcribed oddly (e.g., 'KV cache' -> 'K V cache', 'quantize' -> 'can ties' in one snippet) — the above has been lightly cleaned but retains conversational fragments.

End of transcript.

source.header:
Date: 2025-05-02 | Start: 16:30 PDT | Duration: 48m
Attendees: Maya Chen (Redwood AE), Ethan Park (Redwood SE), Oliver James (Nimbus CTO), Priya Kapoor (Nimbus Head of ML), Sam Torres (Nimbus SRE), Lina Moreno (Nimbus Security)
source.summary: Discovery call focused on reliability, incident tolerance, and multi-region fallback strategies for Nimbus Health. Key asks: active-active vs active-passive routing, SLA expectations for Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private, testing approach for failover, data residency and KMS concerns. Agreed next steps: share security pack, run a short failover sim, and exchange benchmark template.
topics: SLA/RTO/RPO expectations for Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private
canary rollouts and automatic fallback to earlier model versions
security controls: KMS, audit logs, SOC2, network topology
source.transcript:
[00:00] Maya (Redwood AE): Hey everyone, thanks for joining — we got about 45, 50 minutes on the calendar, we'll try to keep to the agenda. Quick intros?
[00:12] Oliver (Nimbus CTO): Oliver here, CTO at Nimbus. We're evaluating options for inference serving for our patient-facing summarization service — very sensitive to downtime.
[00:25] Priya (Head of ML): Priya, I run ML. Main concerns: latency SLOs, model quality regressions during failover, and data residency for PHI.
[00:38] Sam (SRE): Sam, infra. I'm focused on runbooks, RTO, how automatic the failover is, and how much manual ops you'll need.
[00:45] Lina (Security): Lina, security. We'll want network diagrams, KMS details, audit logs — SOC two stuff.
[00:51] Maya: Great, thanks. Ethan from our SE team will cover technical details and failover options.
[00:56] Ethan (Redwood SE): Hi all. Quick outline: we can do hosted API, dedicated capacity, or private (VPC/on-prem). For reliability we support several routing policies — active-active multi-region, active-passive with health checks and cold-standby, and per-request fallback to a lower-cost or quantized model. I'll walk through examples.
[01:18] Oliver: When you say active-active, do you mean full state sync for KV caches? Our workload uses long context and KV cache reuse is important.
[01:30] Ethan: Good question. We do not replicate in-memory KV caches across regions in real time — that's expensive and often unnecessary. Active-active is typically at the request-routing level: traffic split with sticky routing and regional caches warmed per region. For session-level cold starts we provide prefix caching and warm pools. If you need cross-region KV mirroring, that's a custom setup and we can discuss tradeoffs.
[01:55] Priya: So if an entire region goes down, is there an automatic fallback to another region? How seamless is it for the end user?
[02:05] Ethan: Yes, you can configure failover policies. Options: immediate failover (route to alternate region as soon as health check fails) which preserves availability but may increase latency, or deferred failover where the system retries local region a few times before switching. There's also soft-fallback — route a subset of traffic while gradually shifting. For session affinity, you might see state loss unless you use a shared persistent context store.
[02:35] Sam: How do you detect a region outage? Health checks or cloud provider signals?
[02:40] Ethan: Both. We integrate cloud health APIs where possible, run active health probes from multiple vantage points, and surface internal node health. There's a policy layer that decides thresholds (consecutive failed probes, tail latency spikes).
[02:52] Oliver: SLA question: what's your uptime guarantee for Hosted vs Dedicated?
[02:58] Maya: Hosted tiers have a published SLO — it's in our docs — but for enterprise customers we move to Dedicated with SLA attachments. Dedicated customers get a higher committed uptime and prioritized capacity. For private deployments, SLAs are usually contractual and dependent on the deployment model (we support VPC with private control plane).
[03:18] Lina: From security perspective, if traffic shifts to another region, how do you handle data residency and PHI controls?
[03:27] Ethan: Routing policies can be geo-constrained. So you can block failover out of region for PHI-sensitive routes. Alternatively, for high-availability while preserving residency, we recommend a private deployment with regional on-prem nodes or a Dedicated capacity provisioned in the same legal region.
[03:48] Priya: Hmm, okay. What about model versioning during failover — if the alternate region has a different model variant or quantized version, how do you guarantee quality?
[03:58] Ethan: We support fallback to compatible variants. You can pin model family/compatibility levels, and the system will only fall back to variants that pass a compatibility matrix. We encourage running preflight checks — Redwood can run A/B regressions; Redwood Optimize gives suggested quantization profiles per workload.
[04:20] Oliver: Any out-of-the-box controls for throttling or graceful degradation? If latency spikes we want to degrade gracefully rather than error out.
[04:29] Ethan: Yes: token-level budgeters, rate-limiters per API key, and structured graceful degradation policies (e.g., switch to shorter max tokens, lower-quality model, or cached prefix responses).
[04:41] Sam: Can you automate a rollback if the fallback variant shows poor quality?
[04:45] Ethan: We have runtime quality hooks. If you provide a small evaluation set, we can continuously score outputs and automatically revert to the prior model or region if quality falls below a threshold. That ties into our rollouts and canary tooling.
[04:58] [05:00] Oliver: (murmur) Sounds promising. Can you talk about warm pools and cold starts? We see big latency hits when instances scale from zero.
[05:12] Ethan: Absolutely. Dedicated and Private allow warm pools — you can keep a minimum number of warm workers per region to meet p99 latency budgets. Hosted has autoscaling with burst capacity, but cold starts are more possible unless you pay for pre-warmed capacity. Warm pools are our recommended approach for very low p99 targets.
[05:36] Priya: For pilot we probably need p50 ~ 200ms, p99 < 1s for short summaries. Any examples of customers hitting those numbers?
[05:46] Ethan: Yes, we have several references — we'll send anonymized latency profiles. Those numbers are achievable with dedicated pools and optimized kernels. We'll attach a spreadsheet.
[05:58] Maya: Lina, any specific audit/KMS asks now?
[06:03] Lina: Two things: network flow logs retained for at least 1 year, and KMS integration with our key policy. Also, HSM support for very sensitive keys. We want to see where logs are stored and who has access.
[06:17] Ethan: We integrate with customer-managed KMS for Private. For Hosted and Dedicated we support CMK via your cloud provider KMS; HSM can be supported for Private. Audit logs include token-level traces, model IDs, and request metadata. We can redact PII at ingest if needed.
[06:35] Oliver: On the runbook side: after a region outage, what's the recommended manual checklist vs automated actions?
[06:42] Ethan: Automated: health detection, automatic routing according to failover policy, and alerting. Manual: validate stateful sessions, check KV consistency (if you use external store), and validate quality of the fallback region. We provide a post-mortem template.
[06:58] Sam: Do you support chaos testing or scheduled failovers? We'd like to run simulated region failures.
[07:05] Ethan: Yes — we can schedule a controlled failover window. For Hosted customers we simulate traffic shift using Canary with a shadow region. For Dedicated we can do a more invasive simulation. We'll coordinate with your change window.
[07:22] Priya: We care about percent of traffic that will be lost during failover. Any numbers?
[07:27] Ethan: If you use active-active with sticky sessions and warm pools, expected user-facing error rate is near-zero, but tail latencies may increase. Active-passive with cold standby might see a brief spike in errors while instances spin up — 30s to a couple minutes depending on warm pool settings. We can tune retry/backoff to reduce visible errors.
[07:49] Oliver: How does the platform handle queued requests during region failover?
[07:54] Ethan: Requests can be retried at the edge for a configurable period; we also support request hedging (send parallel requests to two regions and accept the fastest). Hedging doubles cost for the hedged requests but reduces tail latency and provides resilience.
[08:12] Maya: Cost vs reliability question often comes up — how do you recommend customers decide?
[08:18] Ethan: It's a tradeoff: active-active + hedging is highest cost but best latency and availability. Active-passive with warm pools is mid-cost. Cold-standby is lowest cost but longer RTO. We usually map it to business impact: patient-facing critical flows -> active-active/dedicated; internal batch flows -> cold standby.
[08:36] Priya: For HIPAA/PHI, we will probably require Dedicated or Private. What's the fastest path to prove reliability in a pilot?
[08:45] Maya: A Dedicated pilot is typically quickest — we provision reserved capacity in region, attach an SLA and run the failover scenarios. Private is more complex due to network setup.
[08:56] Lina: If we go Dedicated, can you guarantee no cross-tenant KV leakage?
[09:01] Ethan: Dedicated pools are tenant isolated at the runtime level. KV caches are per-tenant and not shared. We also provide encryption at rest and in-transit, and tenant-scoped audit logs.
[09:14] Oliver: About incident transparency — do you publish regional incident timelines and expected time to restore?
[09:20] Maya: We have status.redwood.ai and we provide incident timelines for customers. For Dedicated/Enterprise we also provide an incident manager and post-mortem SLA attachments as part of the contract.
[09:33] Sam: For the actual failover test, what telemetry do you need from us?
[09:38] Ethan: Ideally: representative requests, session patterns, p50/p90/p99 latencies, and any upstream dependencies like context store endpoints. Also, user traffic distribution so we can emulate load.
[09:51] Priya: We'll need to make sure we capture quality metrics — we don't want fallbacks that increase hallucinations.
[09:57] Ethan: Absolutely. Provide an evaluation set and we'll run automatic regression checks — we can set thresholds for BLEU-like metrics or task-specific scoring. If quality drops, the system can revert.
[10:08] [10:15] Oliver: Quick tangent — what about automatic model downgrades when load is high?
[10:20] Ethan: You can configure graceful degrade rules: e.g., during sustained high load, automatically step down from a large-flops model to a faster quantized model, reduce max tokens, or enable prefix cache responses. These policies are tunable per route.
[10:34] Priya: That helps. For the pilot, we need to see a configuration sample — a policy that says: if p95 latency > 800ms for 2 minutes, switch to backup model and notify SRE.
[10:44] Ethan: I'll attach a sample policy and a short guide on thresholds.
[10:48] Maya: Let's capture any blockers to starting pilot.
[10:52] Sam: Network approvals for Dedicated, and a time window for the failover sim. Also need a legal review for the SLA.
[10:58] Lina: Security review needs the SOC2 pack and network diagram.
[11:02] Maya: We'll get those over.
[11:06] [11:10] Oliver: On the topic of cross-region routing, does Redwood's control plane ever become a single point of failure? If control plane is down, do data plane requests still route?
[11:20] Ethan: Data plane is designed to be autonomous. The control plane manages configs and rollouts; we cache configs on data plane nodes. If control plane is impaired, existing configs continue to run and routing continues. We do recommend multi-region control-plane redundancies for Private deployments if you want to be extra cautious.
[11:38] Priya: How fast do config changes propagate? Rolling updates might affect latency during deploys.
[11:44] Ethan: Typically under 30s to a few minutes depending on rollout strategy. We support canary rollouts to minimize impact. During model swaps we stage KV pre-loads to warm caches.
[11:56] Sam: Any hard limits on how many regions we can serve from simultaneously?
[12:01] Ethan: No hard limit — it's constrained by capacity planning and cost. We typically recommend starting in 2 regions for low-latency global coverage and expand as needed.
[12:12] Maya: Let's talk timeline: Priya, what does your decision timeline look like?
[12:18] Priya: We hope to pick a direction by end of May. Pilot would ideally start mid-May.
[12:24] Maya: Okay, that gives us time to get security docs and a draft pilot plan.
[12:29] [12:35] Oliver: One last nit: for audit logs, how granular are they? We need to trace request -> model ID -> tokens used, but masked PII.
[12:44] Ethan: Logs are token-level metadata and model IDs. We support configurable redaction policies and retention settings. For PHI we can redact full text and store only hashes.
[12:57] Lina: Who has access to raw logs in Hosted? We want separation of duties.
[13:02] Ethan: Access is role-based; we support RBAC and integrations with your SSO (SAML/SCIM). For Dedicated/Private you can limit Redwood's operational access via break-glass workflows.
[13:18] Priya: Great. Can you also describe cost implications of hedging vs warm pools in a short note?
[13:25] Maya: We'll include a cost comparison.
[13:28] Ethan: I'll quantify typical percent cost uplift for hedging (roughly 100% for hedged requests) and warm pools (depends on GPU type and minutes kept warm).
[13:40] [13:45] Oliver: Okay let's summarize next steps.
[13:48] Maya: Sure. Redwood to send SOC2 + network diagram, Ethan to send benchmark template and policy examples, Maya to send pilot commercial outline, Nimbus to provide SLOs and traffic sample. We'll aim to set the failover sim for 2025-05-12 pending approvals.
[14:05] Priya: Sounds good.
[14:07] Sam: Thanks everyone.
[14:10] [14:12] Lina: Thanks.
[14:14] Maya: Thanks all, we'll follow up in email.
[14:18] [END]
notes: Customer leans toward Dedicated for PHI compliance but wants to validate failover behavior first
Prioritize sending security artifacts — blocker for pilot start
Pilot success criteria: p99 latency under 1s, zero untriaged errors during 1-hour failover sim, no quality regressions beyond agreed threshold
